docs.title: oijalgorithms
docs.text: oijalgorithms is a Python package written in C containing algorithms often used in the Junior Polish Olympiad in Informatics as well as its High School counterpart. It was made for learning by writing algorithms in Python or using them as a shortcut instead of implementing them yourself.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: The package requires Python 3 and a C compiler.

docs.title: Download from GitHub
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/igni28/oijalgorithms.git
cd oijalgorithms
python -m pip install -e .

docs.title: Check if it works
docs.text:
Run Python and import the package:

import oijalgorithms

Then check if it works, for example by calling the gcd function:

print(oijalgorithms.gcd(20, 15))

Expected output:

5

There are, of coure more complicated algorithms included like this one:

print(oijalgorithms.kmp_search("haystackneedlehayhayhayneedlehay","needle"))

Which helps when you need to find keywords in a text efficiently, returning:

[8,23]

There is also a built-in tester if you decide to learn and write these algorithms yourself (which is recommended):

def your_prefix_sums(values):
    result = []
    current_sum = 0

    for value in values:
        current_sum += value
        result.append(current_sum)

    return result

print(oijalgorithms.test_your_function(your_prefix_sums, 
        oijalgorithms.prefix_sum, 1000))

Your code has just been tested on a 1000 random lists! see below for results:

{'ok': True, 'tests': 1000, 'function': 'prefix_sum'}

docs.title: gcd(a, b)
docs.text: this function returns the GCD (Greatest Common Denominator) of two integers a and b

docs.title: lcm(a, b)
docs.text: this function returns the LCM (Least Common Multiple) of two integers a and b

docs.title: mod_pow(a, b, m)
docs.text: this fuction returns (a^b)%m it uses a fast exponentiation algorithm to greatly reduce the compute time (from normal O(b) to O(log b)) which can be useful for example for hashing algorithms

docs.title: sieve(n)
docs.text: this function returns all of the prime numbers greater or equal to n it works by using an ancient algorithm called the Sieve of Eratosthenes

docs.title: binary_search(val, x)
docs.text: this function returns the index of the very first occurence of the number x in the list val, or -1 if it can not be found.

docs.title: prefix_sum(val)
docs.text: this function, given the list val, returns a list containing its prefix sums, which means that on the i-th element of the result contains the sum of the i+1 first elements. It is useful for quickly finding the sum of the a thru b-th elements by calculating result[b] - result[a-1]

docs.title: bfs(graph, v)
docs.text: this function traverses a graph where: graph is a list of lists where its i-th element is a list of the neighbors of the i-th vertex, for example: [[1],[]] means a graph made from 0 and 1 with an edge going from 0 to 1 (but not vice versa). It does so using a Breadth-First algorithm and returns a list of distances from the given vertex v.

docs.title: dfs(graph, v)
docs.text: this function traverses a graph where: graph is a list of lists where its i-th element is a list of the neighbors of the i-th vertex, for example: [[1],[]] means a graph made from 0 and 1 with an edge going from 0 to 1 (but not vice versa). It does so using a Depth-First algorithm and returns a list containing the order of visits from the given vertex v.

docs.title: toposort(graph)
docs.text: this function sorts a graph without cycles where: graph is a list of lists where its i-th element is a list of the neighbors of the i-th vertex, for example: [[1],[]] means a graph made from 0 and 1 with an edge going from 0 to 1 (but not vice versa). It does so using topological sorting meaning that a vertex can only be added to the list once all of its predecessors were as well It returns a topologically sorted list of vertices.

docs.title: lis_length(tab)
docs.text: This function returns the length of the Longest Increasing Sequence in a given list tab, using an O(n*log n) approach.

docs.title: kmp_search(text, keyword)
docs.text: This function returns the indexes of occurences of the keyword in a given text using Knuth-Morris-Pratts algorithm. The algorithm works in O(n+m) instead of a naive O(n*m) approach

docs.title: test_your_function(func1, func2, n)
docs.text: Tests your function (func1) against this package's version on n randomly generated tests

docs.title: UnionFind
docs.text: This class implements a UnionFind data structure (known otherwise as Find and Union) This structure stores a partition of elements into disjoint sets. It uses path compression and union by set size to improve performance.

docs.title: UnionFind.__init__(n)
docs.text: Creates a UnionFind structure for elements from 0 to n - 1.

docs.title: UnionFind.find(x)
docs.text: Returns the representative of the set containing a given number x.

docs.title: UnionFind.union(a, b)
docs.text: Merges the sets containing a and b. It returns True if the sets were successfully merged and False if not (meaning that the elements were already in the same one)

docs.title: UnionFind.connected(a, b)
docs.text: Checks whether a and b belong to the same set. Return True if a and b are in the same set, False otherwise.

docs.title: UnionFind.size(x)
docs.text: Returns the size of the set containing a given number x.
